stem: Let \( X \) be the number of successes in [[n]] independent trials, each having success probability \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \) (so \( X \) is binomial). What is the variance of \( X \)?
solution: A binomial variable has variance \( np(1-p) = [[n]] \cdot \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\left(1 - \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the mean \( np \), not the variance.
This omits the factor of [[n]], giving the variance of a single trial.
This uses \( np^2 \) instead of \( np(1-p) \).
This is \( n(1-p) \), mismatching the mean and complement factors.
This uses \( 1 + p \) instead of \( 1 - p \).
This is just the success probability \( p \).

stem: Let \( X \) be the number of successes in [[n]] independent trials, each having success probability \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \) (so \( X \) is binomial). What is the mean (expected value) of \( X \)?
solution: A binomial variable has mean \( np = [[n]] \cdot \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the variance \( np(1-p) \), not the mean.
This is just the per-trial success probability, forgetting to scale by [[n]].
This uses \( n(1-p) \) (the expected number of FAILURES) instead of \( np \).
This inverts the success probability before multiplying.
This adds instead of multiplying.
This squares the probability before scaling by [[n]].

stem: A weighted coin lands heads with probability \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \) on each independent flip. Let \( X \) be the number of the flip on which the first head occurs (so \( X \) is geometric, taking values \( 1, 2, 3, \ldots \)). What is the mean of \( X \)?
solution: For a geometric random variable counting the trial of the first success, the mean is \( \frac{1}{p} = \frac{[[pd]]}{[[pn]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the success probability itself, not the reciprocal.
This is the mean of the related \( \text{Geometric}_0 \) variable (number of FAILURES before the first success), \( \frac{1-p}{p} \), not \( \frac{1}{p} \).
This uses \( 1/p^2 \), confusing the mean formula with part of the variance formula.
This uses \( \frac{1}{1-p} \), the wrong complement in the denominator.
This subtracts 1 without dividing correctly.
This is the variance of the failures-counted geometric variable, not the mean of \( X \).

stem: A weighted coin lands heads with probability \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \) on each independent flip. Let \( X \) be the number of the flip on which the first head occurs (so \( X \) is geometric). What is the variance of \( X \)?
solution: For a geometric random variable counting the trial of the first success, the variance is \( \frac{1-p}{p^2} = \frac{1 - \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}}{\left(\frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]}\right)^2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the mean \( 1/p \), not the variance.
This drops the \( (1-p) \) factor from the numerator entirely.
This forgets to square the denominator.
This inverts which factor is squared, putting \( p \) over \( (1-p)^2 \) instead.
This squares the numerator as well, which is not part of the formula.
This is the binomial-style variance \( p(1-p) \), the wrong named distribution's formula.

stem: A random variable \( X \) follows a Poisson distribution with mean \( \frac{[[lamn]]}{[[lamd]]} \). What is the variance of \( X \)?
solution: A defining property of the Poisson distribution is that its variance always equals its mean: \( \operatorname{Var}(X) = \lambda = \frac{[[lamn]]}{[[lamd]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This squares the mean, confusing Poisson with a distribution whose variance is \( \mu^2 \).
This doubles the mean instead of using the mean directly.
This adds 1 to the mean instead of using it directly.
This inverts the fraction.
This subtracts 1 from the mean instead of using it directly.
This triples the mean instead of using the mean directly.

stem: Let \( X \) be a value chosen uniformly at random from the integers \( 1, 2, \ldots, [[n]] \). What is the mean of \( X \)?
solution: For a discrete uniform variable on \( 1, \ldots, n \), the mean is \( \frac{n+1}{2} = \frac{[[n]]+1}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the \(+1\), which is a common off-by-one error for the discrete (as opposed to continuous) uniform mean.
This is off by using \( n-1 \) instead of \( n+1 \) in the numerator.
This is the formula for the VARIANCE, not the mean.
This is just the maximum value, ignoring the minimum.
This divides by 3 instead of 2.
This divides by 3 without the \(+1\) correction either.

stem: Let \( X \) be a value chosen uniformly at random from the integers \( 1, 2, \ldots, [[n]] \). What is the variance of \( X \)?
solution: For a discrete uniform variable on \( 1, \ldots, n \), the variance is \( \frac{n^2-1}{12} = \frac{[[n]]^2-1}{12} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the mean, not the variance.
This divides by 6 instead of 12.
This uses \( n^2+1 \) instead of \( n^2-1 \) in the numerator.
This squares \( n-1 \) instead of using \( n^2-1 \).
This drops the \(-1\) correction from the numerator.
This divides by 4 instead of 12.

stem: Let \( X \) be a continuous random variable distributed uniformly on the interval \( [[[a]], [[hi]]] \). What is the mean of \( X \)?
solution: For a continuous uniform variable on \( [a,b] \), the mean is the midpoint \( \frac{a+b}{2} = \frac{[[a]] + [[hi]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the variance formula, not the mean.
This is just the width of the interval, not its midpoint.
This halves the width instead of averaging the endpoints.
This is only the right endpoint, ignoring the left.
This is only the left endpoint, ignoring the right.
This divides by 3 instead of 2.

stem: Let \( X \) be a continuous random variable distributed uniformly on the interval \( [[[a]], [[hi]]] \). What is the variance of \( X \)?
solution: For a continuous uniform variable on \( [a,b] \), the variance is \( \frac{(b-a)^2}{12} = \frac{([[hi]] - [[a]])^2}{12} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the mean, not the variance.
This divides by 6 instead of 12.
This forgets to square the width before dividing.
This divides by 4 instead of 12.
This forgets to divide by 12 at all.
This divides by 3 instead of 12.

stem: A sample of [[k]] items is drawn without replacement from a population of [[N]] items, of which [[K]] are defective. Let \( X \) be the number of defective items in the sample (so \( X \) is hypergeometric). What is the mean of \( X \)?
solution: The hypergeometric mean matches the binomial mean formula with \( p = K/N \): \( E[X] = k \cdot \frac{K}{N} = [[k]] \cdot \frac{[[K]]}{[[N]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the population proportion, forgetting to scale by the sample size [[k]].
This is the hypergeometric VARIANCE, not the mean.
This inverts the roles of the sample size and defective count.
This inverts the defective proportion.
This omits the factor of [[k]] and includes an extra complement factor.
This adds the sample size instead of multiplying by the proportion.

stem: A weighted coin lands heads with probability \( \frac{[[pn]]}{[[pd]]} \) on each independent flip. Let \( X \) be the number of the flip on which the [[r]]-th head occurs (so \( X \) is negative binomial). What is the mean of \( X \)?
solution: Waiting for the [[r]]-th success generalizes the geometric mean: \( E[X] = \frac{r}{p} = \frac{[[r]]}{[[pn]]/[[pd]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies by \( p \) instead of dividing by it.
This forgets the factor of \( r \), giving only the mean for a single success.
This is the variance formula, not the mean.
This uses the wrong complement \( 1-p \) in the denominator.
This subtracts \( r \) from the reciprocal probability instead of dividing.
This squares \( r \) instead of using it linearly.
